content: ROS (Robot Operating System) uses a publish-subscribe architecture where nodes communicate via topics. A node publishes sensor data (e.g., /scan for LiDAR at 10-40 Hz) and other nodes subscribe. ROS 2 uses DDS (Data Distribution Service) middleware with QoS profiles for reliable or best-effort delivery. URDF (Unified Robot Description Format) defines robot kinematics in XML.

ROS 2 improvements over ROS 1: Real-time capable (deterministic scheduling), multi-robot support, no single point of failure (no roscore master), DDS middleware for enterprise networking, lifecycle-managed nodes, and native Windows/macOS support. ROS 2 Humble (2022) and Iron (2023) are the current LTS releases with 5-year support.

SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) builds a map of an unknown environment while tracking the robot's position. LiDAR-based SLAM (GMapping, Cartographer) achieves 1-5 cm accuracy indoors. Visual SLAM (ORB-SLAM3, RTAB-Map) uses cameras for 3D mapping. Key challenge: loop closure detection to correct accumulated drift errors over large areas.

Visual-Inertial SLAM fuses camera images with IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope) data for robust tracking at 200-1000 Hz IMU rate. ORB-SLAM3 supports monocular, stereo, and RGB-D cameras with map reuse. Google Cartographer achieves real-time 2D/3D SLAM on embedded hardware. LiDAR SLAM (LOAM, LeGO-LOAM) processes 300,000 points/scan at 10 Hz.

Path planning algorithms: A* finds optimal paths on grids in O(b^d) where b=branching factor and d=depth. RRT (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree) handles high-dimensional spaces for robot arms (6+ DOF) with probabilistic completeness. Potential fields use attractive (goal) and repulsive (obstacles) forces — simple but prone to local minima. D* Lite replans efficiently when the environment changes.

PID control: Output = Kp*e(t) + Ki*integral(e(t)) + Kd*d(e(t))/dt. Proportional (Kp) responds to current error, Integral (Ki) eliminates steady-state error (accumulates past error), Derivative (Kd) dampens oscillations (predicts future error). Tuning: Ziegler-Nichols method starts with Kp only, increases until oscillation, then sets Ki and Kd from the oscillation period.

PID tuning guidelines: Start with Kp=1, Ki=0, Kd=0. Increase Kp until the system oscillates at the ultimate gain (Ku) with period (Tu). Ziegler-Nichols: Kp=0.6*Ku, Ki=2*Kp/Tu, Kd=Kp*Tu/8. Common issues: Integral windup (clamp integral term), derivative kick (apply derivative to measurement, not error), and noise amplification (low-pass filter on derivative).

Sensor fusion combines data from multiple sensors for more accurate state estimation. Kalman filter provides optimal estimation for linear Gaussian systems: predict (motion model) → update (measurement model). Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) linearizes nonlinear systems. Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) handles highly nonlinear systems without Jacobian computation, using sigma points.

Complementary filter for IMU: Fuses gyroscope (good short-term, drifts long-term) with accelerometer (noisy short-term, accurate long-term). Formula: angle = alpha * (angle + gyro * dt) + (1-alpha) * accel_angle, where alpha=0.96-0.98. Simpler than Kalman filter with comparable results for attitude estimation. Runs on microcontrollers at 1000+ Hz.

MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) is a lightweight IoT protocol using publish-subscribe over TCP with 2-byte minimum overhead. QoS levels: 0 (at-most-once, fire and forget), 1 (at-least-once, acknowledged), 2 (exactly-once, 4-step handshake). Supports 10,000+ concurrent connections on a Raspberry Pi. Retained messages provide last-known-good values to new subscribers.

MQTT vs HTTP for IoT: MQTT is 93x faster than HTTP for small messages and uses 11.89x less battery (Stanford study). MQTT header is 2 bytes vs HTTP's ~700+ bytes. MQTT maintains persistent connections ideal for real-time telemetry. Popular brokers: Mosquitto (open source, handles 100K msg/sec), HiveMQ (enterprise, millions of connections), AWS IoT Core (managed).

Edge computing processes data near the sensor to reduce latency from 100-200ms (cloud round-trip) to 1-10ms (local). NVIDIA Jetson Nano (128 CUDA cores, 472 GFLOPS) runs AI inference at the edge for $99. Jetson Orin NX delivers 100 TOPS for advanced robotics. AWS Greengrass and Azure IoT Edge bring cloud ML models to edge devices with local inference.

Digital twins create virtual replicas of physical systems synchronized via real-time sensor data. Update frequency: 1-10 Hz for building HVAC, 100-1000 Hz for manufacturing equipment. Azure Digital Twins uses DTDL (Digital Twins Definition Language). AWS IoT TwinMaker integrates with 3D visualization. Digital twins reduce maintenance costs by 25-30% through predictive analytics.

Actuator types in robotics: Servo motors (precise angle control, 0-180 or 360 degrees, PWM signal 1-2ms), Stepper motors (discrete steps, 1.8 degrees/step = 200 steps/revolution, precise positioning without encoder), Linear actuators (convert rotary to linear motion, 10-1000mm stroke). DC motors with encoders provide continuous rotation with speed/position feedback at 100-10,000 RPM.

Servo motor control uses PWM signals: 50 Hz frequency (20ms period), 1ms pulse = 0 degrees, 1.5ms = 90 degrees, 2ms = 180 degrees. High-torque servos for robotics: 10-25 kg-cm at 6V. Dynamixel smart servos provide RS-485 daisy-chain communication, position/velocity/current feedback, and PID tuning — industry standard for research robots.

Computer vision sensors for robots: Depth cameras (Intel RealSense D435: 1280x720 at 90fps, 0.1-10m range), LiDAR (Velodyne VLP-16: 16 channels, 300K points/sec, 100m range), stereo cameras (ZED 2: 2.2K resolution, 0.3-20m depth). Time-of-Flight (ToF) sensors provide 640x480 depth at 30fps for indoor navigation at lower cost.

LiDAR point cloud processing: Voxel grid downsampling reduces point density (0.01-0.05m leaf size for indoor). Ground plane segmentation using RANSAC with 0.2m distance threshold. Object clustering with Euclidean clustering (0.5m tolerance, 10-25000 point limits). Object detection: PointNet++ processes raw 3D points at 48K points/frame for semantic segmentation.

Reinforcement learning for robot control: Sim-to-real transfer trains policies in simulation (MuJoCo, Isaac Gym running 10,000+ parallel environments on GPU) then deploys on real hardware. Domain randomization varies physics parameters (friction 0.5-1.5, mass +/-20%) during training. PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) is the most popular algorithm for continuous control tasks.

RL for locomotion: Boston Dynamics uses model-based control; recent research (ETH Zurich, UC Berkeley) demonstrates RL policies matching or exceeding hand-tuned controllers. Training time: 10-30 minutes in Isaac Gym (4096 parallel envs on RTX 3090) for quadruped locomotion. Reward shaping: forward velocity + energy penalty + smoothness bonus + stability constraint.

Robot safety standards: ISO 10218-1/2 covers industrial robot safety (speed limits, force limits, protective stops). ISO/TS 15066 defines collaborative robot (cobot) safety: maximum allowable force is 150N for chest contact and 130N for head contact. Power and Force Limiting (PFL) cobots operate at reduced speed (250mm/s max) and force when humans are in the workspace.

Collaborative robot (cobot) capabilities: Universal Robots UR10e has 10 kg payload, 1300mm reach, +/-0.05mm repeatability, and 17 safety functions. Cobots cost $25,000-50,000 vs $100,000-500,000 for industrial robots. Deployment time: 1-2 days vs weeks for traditional robots. ROI typically achieved in 6-12 months for pick-and-place, assembly, and quality inspection tasks.

IoT communication protocols by range: Short-range (<100m): BLE 5.0 (2 Mbps, coin cell battery lasts 2+ years), Zigbee (250 kbps, mesh network, 65K nodes), Z-Wave (100 kbps, home automation). Long-range: LoRaWAN (50 kbps, 15 km rural, 5 km urban), NB-IoT (200 kbps, cellular, deep indoor penetration), Sigfox (100 bps, 50 km, ultra-low power).

IoT device management at scale: OTA (Over-The-Air) firmware updates with A/B partitioning for rollback capability. Device provisioning with X.509 certificates for mutual TLS authentication. Fleet monitoring: heartbeat interval 30-300 seconds, alert on 3+ missed heartbeats. AWS IoT Device Defender audits security policies. Typical fleet: 10,000-1M devices per deployment.

Robot Operating System navigation stack (Nav2 in ROS 2): Takes a map (from SLAM), current pose (from localization), and goal position. Costmap2D creates obstacle and inflation layers. Global planner (NavFn using Dijkstra or A*) plans a path. Local planner (DWB or MPPI) generates velocity commands avoiding dynamic obstacles. Recovery behaviors handle stuck situations.

Embedded systems for IoT: ESP32 (dual-core 240MHz, WiFi+BLE, $3-5, Arduino/MicroPython compatible) handles most IoT sensor nodes. STM32 (ARM Cortex-M, 72-480MHz, $2-10) for industrial applications requiring real-time guarantees. Raspberry Pi 4 (quad-core 1.8GHz, 2-8GB RAM, $35-75) runs full Linux for edge computing and ROS nodes.
